title: Big Tag
description: A two-player tag game with multiple levels, where a big elephant chases a small elephant before the timer runs out.

content.text: Big Tag was a school project from 2024, built by a team of four. I'm writing this in 2026 — the initial commit is fourteen months old as of today — so the details come from memory and the repository.

Big Tag is a two-player tag game: one player is the big elephant, the other is the small elephant. The big elephant has to tag the small one before the timer runs out — survive the clock, and the small elephant wins.
Whatever the credits say, I fully built the tag system, the water system, the main menu, and all five of the maps.
The video shows only two of the five maps — the final version of the game was never pushed to the repository, and it's gone for good. A lesson about version control I only needed to learn once.

The main problem was physics. I had only just started using Unity and didn't yet understand its physics systems, which produced sluggish, unresponsive movement. I worked it into a decent state by the end, but players could still stick to walls.
The water system was the other weak point: it ran entirely on the main thread and was slow — the landing ripples were the most expensive part, even if they did look the part.
Revisiting this concept, I'd fix the physics properly: physics materials for the wall-sticking, and movement logic moved into FixedUpdate where it belongs.
I'd move the water simulation onto the GPU in a shader, and give it a real visual upgrade while I'm there.
The post-processing also needed restraint — most notably on the space map, where excessive bloom produced what looked like flashes of light. You can see it in the video if you know to look.

Two years out, the throughline is clear: this project is where I learned to find my way around Unity's physics systems. Every physics decision I've made since — including the choice to write custom physics where Unity's falls short — traces back to the walls I hit here.
